en.extract:
Speke's weaver (Ploceus spekei) is a familiar East African songbird.
The eyes are pale and the bill is on the large side for a weaver.  Unlike many weavers, it has the same plumage all year.  The adult male is yellow with black throat (edged rusty), face, and bill, and variable black mottling on the back.  The adult female's upperparts are dull olive-gray with dusky brown streaks; the underparts are pale yellow, whiter on the belly and grayer on the flanks.  The juvenile is similar but duller.
The song is variable; one version is transcribed as "pew…pew…tew, chinkichi-chewchew-skerinkitsitew". Calls include harsh chatter at the nest and a sharp "tseep!" 
This species nests in colonies or occasionally singly.  The nest, often woven in an acacia, is spherical with a short entrance tube opening sideways or downward.  Many grass stems project sloppily, sometimes obscuring the shape.  It nests in both the short rainy season and the middle of the long rainy season.  Typical clutches comprise four eggs, which are unmarked deep blue, somewhat pointed at both ends, 24 to 26 mm long and 15 to 17 mm wide.
Speke's weaver is found in northern and eastern Somalia, Ethiopia, Kenya (mostly the central highlands), and north-eastern Tanzania.  It inhabits savanna, bush, agricultural land, and towns. In parts of its range it is common, notably the densely populated area of Nairobi and environs, where it visits bird feeders.
This bird was named in honor of John Speke.


References


External links
Speke's weaver -  Species text in Weaver Watch.

{{Short description|Species of bird}} {{Speciesbox | name = Speke's weaver | status = LC | status_system = IUCN3.1 | status_ref = {{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2016 |title=''Ploceus spekei'' |volume=2016 |article-number=e.T22718929A94602339 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T22718929A94602339.en |access-date=13 November 2021}} | image = Speke's weaver (Ploceus spekei).jpg| image_caption = An adult breeding male at
[[Soysambu Conservancy]], Kenya | genus = Ploceus | species = spekei | authority = ([[Theodor von Heuglin|Heuglin]], 1861) }} '''Speke's weaver''' ('''''Ploceus spekei''''') is a familiar [[East Africa]]n songbird. The eyes are pale and the bill is on the large side for a [[Ploceidae|weaver]]. Unlike many weavers, it has the same plumage all year. The adult male is yellow with black throat (edged rusty), face, and bill, and variable black mottling on the back. The adult female's upperparts are dull olive-gray with dusky brown streaks; the underparts are pale yellow, whiter on the belly and grayer on the flanks. The juvenile is similar but duller.{{cite book | last = Zimmerman | first = Dale A. |author2=Turner, Donald A. |author3=Pearson, David J. | year = 1999 | title = Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania | publisher = Princeton University Press | pages = 248, 536, 541 | isbn = 0-691-01022-6}} The song is variable; one version is transcribed as "''pew…pew…tew, chinkichi-chewchew-skerinkitsitew''". Calls include harsh chatter at the nest and a sharp "''tseep''!" This species nests in colonies or occasionally singly. The nest, often woven in an [[acacia]], is spherical with a short entrance tube opening sideways or downward. Many grass stems project sloppily, sometimes obscuring the shape. It nests in both the short rainy season and the middle of the long rainy season. Typical clutches comprise four eggs, which are unmarked deep blue, somewhat pointed at both ends, 24 to 26 mm long and 15 to 17 mm wide.{{cite journal | last = Fuggles-Couchman | journal = Tanganyika Notes and Records | year = 1946 | title = Further Notes on Nesting Habits and the Breeding Seasons of Some Tanganyika Birds | volume = 4 | first = NR | issue = 21 | pages = 9–18 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=IP0KAAAAIAAJ&q=Tanzania+Notes+and+Records+Speke's+weaver | access-date = 2007-04-08 | doi = 10.1177/002198946900400106| url-access = subscription }} Speke's weaver is found in northern and eastern Somalia, Ethiopia, Kenya (mostly the central highlands), and north-eastern Tanzania.{{cite web | title = Speke's Weaver | publisher = Kenya Birds | url = http://www.kenyabirds.org.uk/weaver-sp.htm | access-date = 2007-04-08}} It inhabits [[savanna]], bush, agricultural land, and towns. In parts of its range it is common, notably the densely populated area of [[Nairobi]] and environs, where it visits bird feeders. This bird was named in honor of [[John Hanning Speke|John Speke]].{{cite book | last = Beolen | first = Bo |author2=Watkins, Michael | title = Whose Bird? Common Bird Names and the People They Commemorate | year = 2004 | publisher = Yale University Press | page = 308 | isbn = 0-300-10359-X }} File:A Speke's Weaver.jpg|Adult male ([[Ngorongoro Conservation Area|Ngorogoro]], Tanzania) File:Ploceus RWD3, crop.jpg|Immature bird with dark eye Image:Spekes Weaver female RWD.jpg|Adult male in eclipse plumage File:Speke's Weavers feeding.jpg|A group of Speke's weavers feeding, in Ngorogoro, Tanzania ==References== {{Commons category|Ploceus spekei}} ==External links== * [http://weavers.adu.org.za/sp.php?spp=3934 Speke's weaver] - Species text in Weaver Watch. {{Ploceidae}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q842310}} [[Category:Ploceus|Speke's weaver]] [[Category:Eastern Afromontane endemic bird species]] [[Category:Birds described in 1861|Speke's weaver]] [[Category:Taxa named by Theodor von Heuglin]] {{Ploceidae-stub}}
